summary: My early inspiration to create art came from books by F. Hopkinson Smith. His works, along with beautifully illustrated magazines and traditional artists' tools, shaped my boyhood notions of art. Smith's influence was part of the foundation that led me to explore various mediums and eventually collage.

An impressive trip to Chicago during my youth left a lasting impression on me. That visit was part of the early experiences that fueled my interest in art and culture. Chicago, with its vibrant urban environment, was a formative place in my artistic development.

Vanity Fair was among the beautifully illustrated magazines that inspired me in my early years. Its rich visual content contributed to my fascination with art and design. The magazine played a role in shaping my aesthetic sensibilities before I fully embarked on my artistic career.

Olivet College was a significant place in my formative years, known for its celebrated visitors who enriched the cultural atmosphere. My time there exposed me to a variety of artistic influences and ideas. The college environment helped nurture my creative ambitions.

Joseph Brewer's collections of art and art books were instrumental in deepening my appreciation for art. His assemblage provided me with access to a wide range of artistic expressions and knowledge. Brewer's influence helped me understand the breadth and depth of art history and practice.

In the 1940s, I was closely connected with Bay Area artists, which was a vibrant period for artistic collaboration and experimentation. The Bay Area scene encouraged me to work in several mediums concurrently and explore new artistic directions. This community played a key role in my development as an artist.

In 1951, I had a solo exhibition at the Santa Barbara Museum of Art, which was a milestone in my career. This opportunity allowed me to showcase my work to a broader audience and marked a period when I was beginning to diminish my use of oils. The museum's support was important in my artistic journey.

During my artistic explorations, I acquired a second collection of antique papers in Florence, which influenced my collage work. Florence provided a rich cultural backdrop that deepened my appreciation for materials and traditional art forms. This experience helped solidify collage as my favored medium.

I worked with the Geddis-Martin Gallery in Santa Barbara, which was one of several galleries that represented my work. Their support helped me reach audiences interested in contemporary collage and mixed media. This relationship was part of my broader engagement with galleries across the country.

The Duveen-Graham Gallery in New York was another important venue for my work. Collaborating with them allowed me to connect with the New York art scene and present my collages to a wider audience. Their role was crucial in expanding my professional network.

Edith Halpert's Downtown Gallery in New York was a key gallery where I exhibited. Working with Edith Halpert connected me to a pioneering figure in the art world and helped position my work among other significant modern artists. The gallery was a vital platform for my career advancement.

Bertha Lewinson in Los Angeles was one of the dealers who supported my work. Her involvement helped me establish a presence on the West Coast art scene. Lewinson's support was part of a network of dealers that facilitated my exhibitions and sales.

Esther Bear in Santa Barbara was another important figure who represented my work. Her gallery provided opportunities to exhibit and connect with collectors in the region. Bear's support contributed to my visibility in the Southern California art community.

Rex Evans in Los Angeles was among the dealers I worked with to promote my art. His involvement helped me navigate the commercial aspects of the art world. Evans was part of the network that supported my exhibitions and sales in Los Angeles.

Jodi Scully in Los Angeles was another dealer who played a role in representing my work. Through her, I was able to reach audiences interested in collage and mixed media. Scully's gallery was part of the broader ecosystem supporting my artistic career.

The Staempfli Gallery in New York was one of the galleries that exhibited my work. Their support helped me maintain a presence in the competitive New York art market. Working with Staempfli was important for my career development and exposure.

I also worked with the Springer Gallery in Berlin, which expanded my reach into the European art scene. Exhibiting there allowed me to connect with international audiences and collectors. This relationship was part of my broader global engagement as an artist.

The Sagittarius Gallery in Rome was another venue that represented my work. Exhibiting in Rome connected me with the rich artistic traditions of Italy and a diverse audience. This gallery was part of my international exhibition history.

Antonio Souza in Mexico City was among the dealers who supported my work. His gallery helped me establish connections within the Mexican art community. Souza's involvement was part of my broader international network of galleries and dealers.

Neil McRoberts in London was another dealer who represented my work. Through his gallery, I was able to reach audiences in the UK and Europe. McRoberts' support was important in maintaining my international presence.

Kurt Schwitters was a major influence on my collage work, especially in terms of scale and approach. His innovative techniques inspired me to explore collage as a favored medium. Schwitters' legacy helped justify and shape my own artistic procedures.

Pablo Picasso's collage work was among those I studied and admired. His pioneering use of collage alongside Georges Braque opened new possibilities for artists like myself. Picasso's contributions helped me understand the potential of collage beyond traditional painting.

Georges Braque, along with Picasso, was a key figure in the development of collage. His work demonstrated how collage could be integrated into fine art, which influenced my own practice. Braque's innovations helped me see collage as a serious artistic medium.

Max Ernst's collage work was part of the tradition that influenced me. His surrealist approach expanded the expressive possibilities of collage. Ernst's art encouraged me to experiment with materials and composition in new ways.

Anne Ryan was another collage artist whose work I respected. Her delicate and thoughtful collages provided a model for exploring texture and form. Ryan's contributions helped me appreciate the subtlety possible within the collage medium.

Alberto Burri's work in collage and mixed media was influential in my understanding of materiality. His innovative use of unconventional materials inspired me to pursue a more materials-oriented approach. Burri's art helped me move beyond traditional descriptive images.

Jean Varda was among the collage artists whose work I admired. His vibrant and dynamic compositions encouraged me to explore color and texture in my own collages. Varda's art was part of the broader collage tradition that informed my practice.

Joseph Cornell's collage and assemblage work was an important reference for me. His poetic and imaginative approach showed how collage could convey narrative and emotion. Cornell's art inspired me to consider the storytelling potential of materials.

Saul Steinberg's collage work was part of the artistic milieu that influenced me. His unique style and use of collage elements contributed to my understanding of the medium's versatility. Steinberg's work helped me appreciate the blend of humor and sophistication possible in collage.
